title: The Magic Scythe
summary: A day-laborer receives a magical scythe but loses it by not following instructions.
full_text:
In a remote corner of Iceland, where the winds howl across vast lava fields and the mountains rise like ancient giants against the sky, there once lived a poor farmer. His farm was small and rocky, yielding barely enough barley to feed his family through the harsh northern winter. His fields were thin, his livestock meager, and his heart grew heavy each spring as he contemplated another season of backbreaking labor with little reward.

One summer, as the farmer walked through his sparse fields at dusk, he noticed a strange glow emanating from the edge of his property. Following the light, he came upon a clearing he had never seen before, bathed in an ethereal luminescence. In the center stood a figure, tall and slender, dressed in shimmering gray robes. This was one of the huldufolk, the hidden people who dwell in the rocks and hills of Iceland. The elf spoke in a voice like wind through dry grass.

"I have watched you struggle year after year," the elf said. "Your hands are calloused, your back is bent, and yet you never complain. I will lend you a gift." The elf held out a scythe, its blade gleaming with otherworldly light. "Use this scythe for your harvest. It will cut your grain by itself while you rest. But there is one condition: you must return it to this spot at the end of each day. If you break this promise, the scythe will bring you ruin."

The farmer accepted the scythe with trembling hands. The next morning, he laid it upon the unharvested barley. To his amazement, the scythe rose into the air and began cutting the grain on its own, moving faster and more precisely than any human could. It worked tirelessly through the day, and by evening, every stalk lay neatly bundled. The farmer returned the scythe as promised.

Day after day, the magic scythe worked wonders. The farmer's harvest was bountiful beyond anything he had ever known. His family ate well, his livestock thrived, and for the first time in years, he felt hope. But then a thought crept into his mind like a worm into an apple: what if he kept the scythe? He could use it year after year without condition.

One evening, instead of returning the scythe, he hid it in his barn. That night, the scythe began to move on its own. It flew out of the barn and began cutting everything in sight, not just grain but fences, trees, and even the walls of the farmer's house. The farmer ran in terror as the scythe chased him across his fields. He understood then that he had broken the trust of the hidden people, and there was no escaping the consequences.

At last, the farmer reached the elf's clearing and fell to his knees, begging forgiveness. The elf appeared, sighed, and spoke a single word. The scythe stopped mid-swing and fell to the ground, its glow extinguished. It had become an ordinary tool of iron and wood.

"I gave you a gift freely," said the elf, "but you could not honor the condition. Now the magic is gone forever." The elf vanished, and the clearing returned to darkness. The farmer returned to his ordinary life, his fields smaller than before, his family poorer, but he had learned a lesson he would never forget: the most precious gifts are those that come with trust, and once trust is broken, the magic cannot be restored.
moral: Follow instructions when receiving supernatural gifts.

title: The Day-Labourer and the Hidden People
summary: A poor youth is helped by magical hidden people after showing kindness to their dog.
full_text:
In the rugged landscape of nineteenth-century Iceland, where volcanic mountains rise from vast lava fields and the North Atlantic winds never cease, there lived a poor day-labourer named Jon. He was the son of a farmer who had lost his land, and he worked wherever he could find employment, doing the hardest tasks for the smallest wages. He was known for his kindness, though it often seemed to go unrewarded.

One autumn, Jon was hired to travel north to a distant farm, where he was to help with the harvest. His journey took him through barren valleys and across freezing rivers. As he walked, he met three companions on the road who also sought work. They were rough men, quick to mock Jon's gentle nature.

When they reached the farm, the men were given lodging in the barn. That night, while Jon slept, his companions stole most of his possessions, leaving him only a few scraps of food, a dried leg of mutton, and a tattered coat.

In the morning, Jon discovered the theft but said nothing. He set out to find work on his own, trudging across the desolate moorland. As evening fell, he sat down to rest, exhausted and hungry. A large brown dog appeared from behind a rock and approached him. The dog was thin and looked hungry. Jon, despite having little for himself, broke off a piece of his dried mutton and gave it to the dog. The dog wagged its tail and trotted away, then stopped and looked back at Jon, as if inviting him to follow.

Jon followed the dog across the moor until they reached a grassy hollow where a handsome farmhouse stood, a place Jon had never seen before, though he knew this land well. The dog led him to the door, and a man of noble bearing invited him inside. The man was dressed in fine gray clothes and spoke with a quiet authority.

"Your kindness to my dog has not gone unnoticed," said the man. "I need a labourer who can finish the work on my farm before the winter snows. If you succeed, I will reward you well."

Jon agreed and set to work with a will. He discovered that this was no ordinary farm. The tools never broke, the animals understood every word he spoke, and the fields yielded their harvest as if by magic. He finished the work in half the expected time.

On his departure, the man gave him two casks of butter, sixteen fat sheep, and a small bag of silver coins. "You have been honest and kind," said the man. "You have earned this."

Jon returned home, his riches far exceeding anything he had ever possessed. He was greeted with wonder and respect. And when he passed the place where the handsome farmhouse had stood, he saw only bare rock and moss. For the man was one of the huldufolk, the hidden people of Iceland, and the brown dog had been the test of Jon's character. The hidden people reward those who are kind without expectation, and they remember those who share their last morsel with a hungry beast. Jon married the man's youngest daughter, who was revealed to be one of the hidden folk herself, and they lived in prosperity, Jon having proven that the smallest acts of kindness can lead to the greatest rewards.
moral: Kindness to strangers leads to supernatural rewards.
